hero.name: Feng Liu
hero.motto: At the intersection of systems, signals, and the mind.
hero.school_short: Schaefer School of Engineering and Science

about.heading: About
bio: Dr. Feng Liu is an Assistant Professor of Systems Engineering at Stevens Institute of Technology. Dr. Liu was a Postdoctoral Research Fellow at Harvard Medical School from 2018 to 2020. In the meantime, Dr. Liu was research affiliate at Picower Institute for Learning and Memory at MIT and Martinos Center for Biomedical Imaging at MGH. Dr. Liu received his Ph.D. degree from the University of Texas at Arlington in Industrial Engineering in 2018 with a focus on machine learning for healthcare.

His research interests include brain network analysis, brain imaging, inverse problem, health informatics, and dynamic system. Dr. Liu is the winner of the Best Paper Award at 11th International Conference of Brain Informatics in 2018, and the Best Paper Award of INFORMS Data Mining Society in 2019.
Dr. Liu's research group has received multiple grants from NIH (NINDS, NIBIB, BRAIN Initiative), DoD and New Jersey Health Foundation as PI, MPI, or Co-I.

interests.heading: Research Interests
interests.intro: Working at the boundary of engineering, mathematics, and neuroscience — decoding the brain through signals, networks, and dynamics, and translating those models into clinical decision support.

primary.label: AI for Healthcare
primary.brief: Deep models & LLMs for clinical decision support.

primary.label: Brain Imaging
primary.brief: EEG / MEG / fMRI source imaging and inverse problems.

primary.label: Epilepsy
primary.brief: Seizure semiology, source localization, epileptogenic zone.

primary.label: Optimization
primary.brief: Knot selection, combinatorial search, industrial ops.

project.heading: Highlighted Project
project.title: First fine-tuned large language model for seizure semiology interpretation and epileptogenic-zone prediction
project.body: In collaboration with clinical partners, our group developed the first fine-tuned large language model to interpret seizure semiology and localize the epileptogenic zone — turning unstructured clinical narratives into actionable diagnostic signals for epilepsy surgery evaluation.

students.heading: PhD Students
